title: Why you read an enforcement action

spec.prompt: You read a crypto exchange enforcement action to strengthen your own program. What should your primary goal be while reading it?

options.text: Extract the transferable control lesson and check whether your own program has the same gap.
options.why: The transferable control lesson is what actually improves your own program.

options.text: Memorize the penalty figure and the named executives so you can quote the matter in future training decks.
options.why: Penalty figures and names are trivia, not a test of your controls.

options.text: Confirm the firm operated in a different jurisdiction from yours.
options.why: Jurisdiction differences do not exempt you from the same gap.

options.text: Note the settlement date so you can track the timeline.
options.why: The settlement date has no bearing on your control gaps.

title: Spotting the control failure in the text

spec.scenario: A regulator's published action describes a crypto business that grew quickly.
spec.prompt: Which statements in the action point to an actual control failure you should test your own program against?

items.label: The firm processed transactions for years with no automated monitoring rules configured.
items.why: Years without monitoring rules is a clear control failure.

items.label: The firm let accounts transact before identity verification was completed.
items.why: Transacting before verification defeats the onboarding control.

items.label: The firm never assigned anyone to review screening alerts.
items.why: Unassigned alert review means screening is not really operating.

items.label: The firm was headquartered in a major financial center.
items.why: Location alone is not a control failure.

items.label: The firm expanded its user base rapidly in its first two years.
items.why: Growth by itself does not indicate a failed control.

spec.prompt: Order the steps for reading an enforcement action so it improves your program.

steps.label: Identify the document type and the authority that issued it.
steps.why: Document type sets how much weight the findings carry.

steps.label: Find the specific conduct and the control that failed.
steps.why: You must locate the failed control before anything else.

steps.label: Trace the root cause behind that failed control.
steps.why: Root cause tells you what actually needs fixing.

steps.label: Map the transferable lesson onto a test of your own program.
steps.why: The whole point of reading is to test your own program.

spec.prompt: A mixing service and its builders both face enforcement. Sort each consequence into the track it belongs to.

items.label: The service's addresses are added to a sanctions list, so dealing with them is prohibited.
items.why: Designation restricts dealings, which is a sanctions consequence.

items.label: The individuals who built and ran the service are criminally charged.
items.why: Charging the builders is the criminal track.

items.label: A screening obligation now attaches to any exposure to the listed addresses.
items.why: The screening duty flows from the designation.

items.label: Prosecutors allege the operators knowingly moved criminal proceeds.
items.why: Proving intent to launder is a prosecution element.

items.label: A later court and agency decision revisits whether the addresses stay listed.
items.why: Delisting revisits the sanctions listing itself.

spec.prompt: Match each term to its correct description.

pairs.right: Adding an obfuscation service to a sanctions list so dealings with it are restricted
pairs.why: A designation restricts dealing with the service.

pairs.right: Removing an entry from a sanctions list after review
pairs.why: Delisting is removal from the list after review.

pairs.right: Criminal charges against the people who built and ran a service
pairs.why: Prosecution targets the human operators.

pairs.right: Detecting when a customer's funds touched a sanctioned service
pairs.why: Screening detects contact with a sanctioned service.

spec.prompt: Blockchain analytics flags that a customer withdrawal passed through a sanctioned mixing service two hops before reaching you. What is the correct first response?

options.text: Treat it as sanctions exposure, hold pending review, and escalate per your screening procedures.
options.why: Indirect exposure is still sanctions exposure to escalate.

options.text: Ignore it, since the customer did not personally operate the sanctioned mixing service and cannot fairly be blamed.
options.why: Operating the mixer is not required for exposure to matter.

options.text: Clear it automatically because the funds moved through more than one address.
options.why: Extra hops do not clear sanctions exposure.

options.text: Reclassify the customer as low risk since mixing is a privacy feature.
options.why: Mixer exposure raises risk, it does not lower it.

spec.prompt: Match each control area to the failure a large exchange enforcement matter typically reveals.

pairs.right: Users transacted with little or no verified identity
pairs.why: Weak identity checks are the KYC failure.

pairs.right: Customers in prohibited jurisdictions were not blocked
pairs.why: Unblocked prohibited jurisdictions is a screening failure.

pairs.right: Suspicious activity produced few or no reports
pairs.why: Few reports despite volume is a monitoring failure.

pairs.right: The business served users without registering where required
pairs.why: Serving users unregistered is a perimeter failure.

spec.scenario: A crypto exchange is onboarding users very quickly across many markets.
spec.prompt: Which observations signal the scale-without-controls failure mode?

items.label: User growth is far outpacing investment in compliance staffing and tooling.
items.why: Growth outpacing compliance is the core failure mode.

items.label: Leadership treats compliance obligations as optional in high-growth markets.
items.why: Treating obligations as optional is a governance failure.

items.label: Sanctions and monitoring controls have not been extended to new products.
items.why: New products without controls widen the gap.

items.label: The exchange publishes a quarterly transparency report.
items.why: A transparency report is not a control failure.

items.label: The exchange lists a large number of trading pairs.
items.why: Many trading pairs alone is not a control failure.

spec.prompt: After a major resolution, sort each remediation measure by the type of gap it addresses.

buckets.label: Fixes a governance or accountability gap

items.label: Appoint an independent compliance monitor to oversee the program.
items.why: A monitor addresses accountability and oversight.

items.label: Give the compliance function real authority and board reporting lines.
items.why: Compliance authority and board lines are governance.

items.label: Configure transaction-monitoring rules and staff an alert-review team.
items.why: Monitoring rules and reviewers are operational controls.

items.label: Deploy sanctions screening across all customer jurisdictions.
items.why: Screening deployment is an operational control.

items.label: Establish clear senior accountability for AML outcomes.
items.why: Senior accountability is a governance fix.

spec.prompt: A derivatives platform served customers in a regulated market but ran no AML program and required almost no identity verification. What is the core failure?

options.text: It operated inside a regulated perimeter without the minimum required AFC program.
options.why: Being in scope with no program is the core failure.

options.text: It offered leveraged products that some retail customers did not fully understand or use safely.
options.why: Product complexity is a conduct issue, not the AML failure.

options.text: It used an offshore corporate structure to hold its operating entity.
options.why: Corporate structure is not the AML program gap.

options.text: It grew faster than its competitors in the same market.
options.why: Growth speed is not itself the failure.

spec.prompt: Order the steps to stand up a minimum viable AFC program for an exchange that has none.

steps.label: Determine the regulatory perimeter and where you must register.
steps.why: The perimeter defines your obligations first.

steps.label: Build customer onboarding with identity verification.
steps.why: Onboarding verification is the entry control.

steps.label: Add transaction monitoring and sanctions screening.
steps.why: Monitoring and screening watch ongoing activity.

steps.label: Assign accountability and file reports when required.
steps.why: Accountability and reporting close the loop.

spec.scenario: You are reviewing a fast-growing platform that leadership proudly calls compliance-light.
spec.prompt: Which findings are genuine red flags of a missing AML program?

items.label: Accounts can trade at full size with no identity verification.
items.why: No verification at full size is a KYC failure.

items.label: No one is designated as responsible for AML controls.
items.why: No AML owner is a real program gap and a liability risk.

items.label: No suspicious activity has ever been reported despite high volumes.
items.why: Zero reports at high volume signals broken monitoring.

items.label: The platform uses a well-known cloud hosting provider.
items.why: A cloud provider is not a control failure.

items.label: The platform offers customer support in several languages.
items.why: Multilingual support is not a control failure.

spec.prompt: Sort each item by whether it is a laundering technique the illicit actor uses or a defensive control the program uses.

buckets.label: Control the program uses to counter it

items.label: Passing stolen funds through mixers and tumblers to break the trail.
items.why: Mixers are a laundering technique.

items.label: Rapid cross-chain and cross-border hops to obscure origin.
items.why: Cross-border hops obscure origin, a technique.

items.label: Screening incoming deposits against designated addresses.
items.why: Screening deposits is a defensive control.

items.label: Behavioral transaction monitoring tuned for rapid layering.
items.why: Behavioral monitoring is a defensive control.

items.label: Chaining many freshly created wallets to fragment the flow.
items.why: Chaining fresh wallets is a laundering technique.

spec.prompt: Match each term to its correct description.

pairs.right: A nation-state directing hacks of crypto platforms to raise funds
pairs.why: A state directing hacks is state-sponsored theft.

pairs.right: Raising or moving funds to support weapons programs
pairs.why: Funding weapons programs is proliferation financing.

pairs.right: Wallets placed on a sanctions list that must not be dealt with
pairs.why: Listed wallets must not be dealt with.

pairs.right: The duty to block and report dealings with sanctioned parties
pairs.why: Blocking and reporting is the core sanctions duty.

spec.prompt: Order the steps to investigate a suspected state-linked theft flowing into your platform.

steps.label: Detect the inbound deposit through a monitoring or screening alert.
steps.why: Detection is where the case starts.

steps.label: Trace the funds back across hops to the source of the theft.
steps.why: Tracing back finds the source.

steps.label: Check the trail against designated addresses and known typologies.
steps.why: Checking lists and typologies confirms the risk.

steps.label: Freeze, report to the relevant authority, and preserve the evidence.
steps.why: Freezing and reporting is the required action.

spec.prompt: A new customer deposits from a wallet with recent indirect exposure to a sanctioned mixer, uses a self-hosted wallet, and gives a vague source of funds. What is the best integrated response?

options.text: Apply enhanced due diligence, document source of funds, and set monitoring before deciding.
options.why: EDD, documentation, and monitoring is the balanced response.

options.text: Reject immediately, because any indirect exposure to a mixer always means the customer is a criminal actor.
options.why: Indirect exposure alone does not prove criminality.

options.text: Approve with standard onboarding since self-hosted wallets are common and low risk.
options.why: Standard onboarding ignores the combined risk signals.

options.text: Approve but flag the account for closure in twelve months regardless of findings.
options.why: A fixed closure date is not risk-based judgment.

spec.scenario: You are triaging one onboarding case under time pressure.
spec.prompt: Which factors genuinely raise the customer's risk and warrant escalation?

items.label: The deposit traces two hops from a sanctioned service.
items.why: Two hops from a sanctioned service is real exposure.

items.label: The stated source of wealth cannot be reconciled with on-chain history.
items.why: Unreconcilable source of wealth is a genuine flag.

items.label: The customer resists explaining the purpose of the account.
items.why: Refusing to explain purpose raises risk.

items.label: The customer uses a hardware wallet to hold their assets.
items.why: A hardware wallet is a normal custody choice.

items.label: The customer asks about withdrawal fees during onboarding.
items.why: Asking about fees is ordinary customer behavior.

spec.prompt: Match each recurring failure mode to the control that would have prevented it.

pairs.right: Mapping and respecting the regulatory perimeter
pairs.why: Perimeter mapping prevents unregistered operation.

pairs.right: Enforced KYC at account opening
pairs.why: Enforced KYC prevents unverified onboarding.

pairs.right: Screening deposits and withdrawals against lists
pairs.why: Screening prevents missed sanctioned exposure.

pairs.right: Staffed monitoring with escalation and reporting
pairs.why: Staffed monitoring prevents unworked alerts.

spec.prompt: A regulator asks your team to identify which of these actors is a virtual asset service provider (VASP) subject to AML obligations. Which one qualifies?

options.text: A custodial exchange that holds customer cryptoassets and executes trades on customers' behalf.
options.why: It conducts a covered activity as a business for and on behalf of others.

options.text: A person who buys crypto solely for their own long-term personal investment.
options.why: Acting only for oneself is not a service provided to others.

options.text: A developer who publishes open-source smart-contract code to a public repository but never takes custody of, controls, or transfers any customer assets.
options.why: Publishing code alone, without any covered activity, is not VASP conduct.

options.text: A merchant that accepts crypto only as payment for its own products and services.
options.why: Accepting payment for one's own goods is not a covered service.

spec.scenario: A compliance analyst reviews recent activity across the firm's crypto ATM network and its over-the-counter (OTC) trading desk.
spec.prompt: Select the genuine AML red flags.

items.label: A single customer makes many just-below-threshold cash deposits at several different crypto ATMs on the same day.
items.why: This pattern suggests structuring to evade reporting thresholds.

items.label: An OTC counterparty refuses to provide any beneficial-owner information for a large trade.
items.why: Refusing beneficial-owner details blocks required due diligence.

items.label: A customer completes standard identity verification and provides a consistent, documented source of funds.
items.why: Complete verification and clear sourcing is expected normal conduct.

items.label: The crypto ATM operator publishes its per-transaction fees on its website.
items.why: Transparent fee disclosure is ordinary business practice.

items.label: A new customer buys crypto at an ATM and immediately sends it to a mixing service.
items.why: Immediate routing to a mixer signals intent to obscure the trail.

spec.prompt: Sort each arrangement by whether an intermediary exists that can screen, freeze, or report activity.

items.label: A centralized custodial exchange holding user funds
items.why: A custodian intermediary can screen and freeze accounts.

items.label: A custodial OTC desk executing trades for clients
items.why: The desk holds funds and can apply controls on clients.

items.label: A crypto ATM operated by a registered money services business
items.why: The registered operator is an intermediary that can apply KYC.

items.label: A peer-to-peer swap between two self-hosted wallets with no intermediary
items.why: No service provider sits between the two parties.

items.label: A fully non-custodial automated exchange protocol with no operator taking custody
items.why: No custodial operator holds funds or controls the flow.

spec.prompt: Match each DeFi concept to its correct description.

pairs.right: A smart-contract reserve of paired tokens that traders swap against
pairs.why: Pools supply the on-chain liquidity that swaps draw from.

pairs.right: A member-governed organization that votes on protocol changes using tokens
pairs.why: DAOs coordinate decisions through token-weighted voting.

pairs.right: Self-executing code that runs automatically when its conditions are met
pairs.why: Smart contracts enforce logic without a human intermediary.

pairs.right: A layering method that moves value through several decentralized services
pairs.why: Sequential swaps fragment the value trail across venues.

spec.prompt: Put the stages of a typical DeFi layering flow in the correct order.

steps.label: Illicit funds enter DeFi through a token swap on a decentralized exchange
steps.why: Entry converts the funds into an on-chain tradable token.

steps.label: Value is routed through a liquidity pool into a different token
steps.why: Swapping tokens begins to distance value from its origin.

steps.label: Assets are moved across several lending and swap protocols to fragment the trail
steps.why: Multiple hops complicate tracing across services.

steps.label: Cleaned value is consolidated and withdrawn to an off-ramp for conversion
steps.why: Consolidation prepares the value to exit for cash-out.

title: When is DeFi an obliged entity?

spec.prompt: Under the FATF 'control or sufficient influence' approach, when might a self-described DeFi arrangement be treated as a VASP and an obliged entity?

options.text: When identifiable persons retain control or sufficient influence over the service, they may qualify as a VASP.
options.why: Owners or operators with control can carry VASP obligations.

options.text: Never, because any arrangement labeled 'decentralized' is automatically outside all AML obligations.
options.why: A label does not determine regulatory status; conduct does.

options.text: Only if the smart contract contains a bug that lets an administrator pause trading during an emergency shutdown of the entire protocol.
options.why: A bug is not the test; ongoing control or influence is.

options.text: Only when the protocol charges no fees to any of its users at any point in time.
options.why: Fee levels are irrelevant to the control-or-influence test.

spec.prompt: Sort each asset by whether a central issuer can freeze or blocklist tokens.

items.label: A fiat-backed stablecoin with a central issuer that maintains a blocklist
items.why: The issuer can block flagged addresses at will.

items.label: A centrally issued tokenized security with a transfer-agent contract
items.why: The transfer agent can restrict or reverse transfers.

items.label: A permissioned stablecoin whose issuer can pause all transfers
items.why: A pause control gives the issuer freeze capability.

items.label: A decentralized native coin with no central issuer
items.why: There is no operator able to freeze holdings.

items.label: A non-custodial NFT minted on a permissionless chain with no admin controls
items.why: No admin key exists to seize or block the token.

spec.scenario: An analyst reviews trading activity across an NFT marketplace for signs of manipulation and laundering.
spec.prompt: Select the genuine red flags.

items.label: The same cluster of wallets repeatedly buys and sells one NFT among themselves at escalating prices.
items.why: Self-dealing among linked wallets indicates wash trading.

items.label: An NFT is sold once at a market-consistent price to an unrelated buyer.
items.why: A single arm's-length sale is ordinary market activity.

items.label: High-value NFT trades are funded from wallets with direct exposure to a sanctioned mixer.
items.why: Funding tied to a mixer signals illicit-source value.

items.label: A creator lists a new collection with publicly disclosed royalty terms.
items.why: Disclosed royalties are a normal, transparent practice.

items.label: Rapid round-trip trades generate volume but no change in net beneficial ownership.
items.why: Volume without ownership change points to wash trading.

spec.prompt: Match each stablecoin concept to its correct description.

pairs.right: Claims one-to-one reserves of cash and cash-equivalents held by an issuer
pairs.why: Its peg rests on held reserves backing each token.

pairs.right: Maintains its peg through code and market incentives rather than reserves
pairs.why: It relies on mechanisms, not custodied backing assets.

pairs.right: Lets a central operator block or seize tokens at a flagged address
pairs.why: This control enables the issuer to halt illicit movement.

pairs.right: A report used to test whether backing claims match actual holdings
pairs.why: Attestations probe whether reserves really exist.

spec.prompt: Put the stages of a common obfuscation flow in the correct order.

steps.label: Illicit funds are deposited into a mixing service to break the deposit-to-withdrawal link
steps.why: Mixing severs the direct on-chain connection first.

steps.label: Withdrawn funds are swapped into a privacy coin to further obscure the trail
steps.why: Privacy coins reduce on-chain visibility of the value.

steps.label: Value is bridged across chains to fragment tracing across separate ledgers
steps.why: Cross-chain hops split the trail over multiple ledgers.

steps.label: Assets are consolidated and cashed out at an off-ramp
steps.why: Cash-out converts the obscured value back to usable funds.

spec.prompt: A customer's incoming deposit was withdrawn from a mixing service shortly before it reached your exchange. What is the most appropriate response?

options.text: Treat the mixer exposure as elevated risk, apply enhanced due diligence, and escalate or report as warranted.
options.why: Mixer exposure raises risk and calls for EDD and reporting.

options.text: Accept the deposit without review because a mixer makes the funds untraceable and therefore low risk.
options.why: Obscured origin raises risk; it does not lower it.

options.text: Immediately close every account that has ever received any transaction indirectly connected to any privacy tool, regardless of context or amount.
options.why: Blanket de-risking ignores risk-based, proportionate handling.

options.text: Ignore the exposure since on-chain analytics can never say anything useful after a mixer.
options.why: Analytics can still surface exposure and next-hop signals.

spec.scenario: An analyst monitors wallet activity for signs that a customer is deliberately obscuring value flows.
spec.prompt: Select the genuine obfuscation red flags.

items.label: A customer rapidly converts incoming funds into a privacy coin and immediately moves them off-platform.
items.why: Fast conversion and exit suggests intent to obscure.

items.label: Funds are chain-hopped across multiple bridges within minutes of receipt for no economic reason.
items.why: Rapid, purposeless bridging is a layering indicator.

items.label: A customer holds a diversified portfolio and trades a few times a month on transparent chains.
items.why: Ordinary, visible activity is not an obfuscation signal.

items.label: Incoming deposits have direct exposure to a known tumbler cluster.
items.why: Direct tumbler exposure ties value to obscuring services.

items.label: A customer occasionally uses a well-known transparent public blockchain for routine transfers.
items.why: Routine transparent-chain use is normal behavior.

spec.prompt: Sort each crypto-related action into the money-laundering stage it best represents.

items.label: Illicit cash is used to buy stablecoins over the counter through an unregistered broker
items.why: Converting dirty cash into crypto is the entry point that introduces illicit value into the system.

items.label: Coins are split across dozens of wallets and swapped through several tokens on a decentralized exchange
items.why: Rapid splitting and swapping is layering designed to obscure the trail.

items.label: Laundered proceeds are cashed out at a compliant exchange and used to buy a legitimately titled property
items.why: Returning cleaned value to the legitimate economy as an asset is integration.

items.label: Funds move through a mixing service and then across a cross-chain bridge to a new blockchain
items.why: Mixers and bridge hops break the on-chain link, a classic layering move.

items.label: Crypto is converted to fiat and paid as an apparently normal salary into a controlled company
items.why: Dressing proceeds as legitimate income reintroduces them as clean funds, which is integration.

spec.scenario: A newly funded customer account at a virtual asset service provider receives a large stablecoin deposit and immediately begins moving value.
spec.prompt: Which behaviors are genuine layering red flags rather than ordinary activity?

items.label: Within minutes, deposits are routed through a mixer and then across three separate cross-chain bridges
items.why: Immediate mixing and multi-chain hopping is a deliberate obfuscation pattern.

items.label: The customer swaps between several low-liquidity tokens on a decentralized exchange with no economic rationale
items.why: Purposeless chain-swapping through DeFi is used to break the transaction trail.

items.label: The customer withdraws a portion to a hardware wallet they control and hold long term
items.why: Self-custody for long-term holding is common and not inherently suspicious.

items.label: Value is split into many equal amounts sent to freshly created wallets seconds apart
items.why: Automated fan-out into new wallets is a layering signature.

items.label: The customer pays a small network fee that matches current market gas prices
items.why: Paying prevailing gas fees is normal and expected.

spec.prompt: Illicit crypto can hop across many wallets, but where does an AML program most reliably identify the beneficiaries behind laundered value?

options.text: At the fiat on-ramp and off-ramp points, where regulated intermediaries perform identity checks
options.why: Regulated ramps are the chokepoints where pseudonymous value meets verified identity.

options.text: At each intermediate self-hosted wallet, because every hop must be individually reported to regulators
options.why: Intermediate self-hosted wallets carry no reporting duty and are pseudonymous.

options.text: During the layering stage, since mixing services are required to keep and share customer records
options.why: Mixers are designed to obscure and typically do not maintain verified records.

options.text: Only on the underlying blockchain, because addresses always resolve automatically to real-world names
options.why: Blockchain addresses are pseudonymous and do not resolve to identities on their own.

spec.prompt: A monitoring system tuned for large-value laundering keeps missing suspected terrorist-financing activity. What best explains the gap?

options.text: Terrorist financing often moves in small, low-value amounts that fall below thresholds set for large laundering flows
options.why: TF value is frequently small, so amount-based thresholds designed for laundering miss it.

options.text: Terrorist financing only ever uses privacy coins, so transparent chains never carry any relevant activity
options.why: TF uses many asset types, including transparent chains, not only privacy coins.

options.text: Terrorist financing is always a single large transfer, so splitting logic is unnecessary for detection
options.why: TF is typically many small flows, not one large transfer.

options.text: Terrorist financing can only ever originate from funds that are themselves the proceeds of a prior serious predicate crime, never from clean or legitimately earned money
options.why: TF can be funded by legitimate sources, unlike laundering which starts with illicit proceeds.

title: Match the financing crime to its signature

spec.prompt: Match each financing concept to the characteristic that best distinguishes it.

pairs.right: Value, which may be legitimately sourced, is directed toward acts of violence
pairs.why: TF is defined by destination and purpose, not by the source being illicit.

pairs.right: Funds support weapons programs in breach of international sanctions and controls
pairs.why: PF concerns funding of weapons of mass destruction programs against sanctions.

pairs.right: A nation-state actor steals crypto to fund its own strategic objectives
pairs.why: This treats large-scale hacking as statecraft to raise revenue.

pairs.right: Illicitly obtained value is disguised so it appears to have a lawful origin
pairs.why: Laundering starts from illicit proceeds and hides their criminal source.

spec.scenario: An analyst reviews a cluster of crypto activity tied to a customer operating across several high-risk corridors.
spec.prompt: Which observations are genuine proliferation or terrorist-financing red flags?

items.label: Wallet exposure traces back to addresses linked to a sanctioned jurisdiction's procurement network
items.why: Exposure to sanctioned procurement infrastructure is a proliferation-financing indicator.

items.label: Frequent small transfers to fundraising addresses previously flagged in violent-extremist appeals
items.why: Repeated small donations to flagged appeal addresses is a TF signature.

items.label: The customer occasionally pays a mainstream merchant for legitimate goods in stablecoins
items.why: Ordinary retail spending is not a financing red flag on its own.

items.label: Structured purchases of dual-use components routed through opaque intermediary wallets
items.why: Concealed dual-use procurement is a proliferation-financing concern.

items.label: The customer holds a diversified portfolio across several major cryptoassets
items.why: Holding a diversified portfolio is normal investor behavior.

spec.prompt: Put the stages of a typical pig-butchering investment scam in the order they occur.

steps.label: The scammer initiates contact through a social or messaging platform and builds a trusting relationship
steps.why: Grooming and trust-building is the entry stage of the scam.

steps.label: The victim is introduced to a fake investment platform showing fabricated but attractive returns
steps.why: The staged platform manufactures false confidence to draw deposits.

steps.label: The victim is encouraged to deposit increasing amounts as the displayed balance appears to grow
steps.why: Fattening the victim escalates the sums before extraction.

steps.label: Withdrawals are blocked behind invented fees and taxes, and the operators disappear with the funds
steps.why: The exit stage extracts the maximum and cuts off the victim.

title: Rug pull or legitimate token launch

spec.scenario: A customer asks about investing in a newly launched token that is being heavily promoted online.
spec.prompt: Which features are genuine rug-pull or Ponzi red flags rather than ordinary launch traits?

items.label: Anonymous developers hold a large concentrated share of supply with no lock-up
items.why: Concentrated insider supply with no lock enables a sudden dump.

items.label: Returns are promised as guaranteed and paid to early holders from new deposits
items.why: Guaranteed returns funded by new money is the Ponzi mechanic.

items.label: The project publishes an audited contract and a public, verifiable team
items.why: Audits and identifiable teams reduce rather than raise suspicion.

items.label: Liquidity can be withdrawn by the creators at any time with no locking mechanism
items.why: Removable liquidity lets creators drain the pool, the core rug-pull risk.

items.label: The token trades on venues with normal spreads and organic volume over time
items.why: Organic, sustained trading is a sign of genuine market activity.

spec.prompt: A victim's stolen crypto lands in an account at your platform and is being moved onward. Why does fraud matter to the AML function, not just fraud prevention?

options.text: Fraud proceeds are criminal property, so moving them through the platform can constitute money laundering that must be detected and reported
options.why: Handling fraud proceeds triggers AML obligations because they are criminal property.

options.text: Fraud is handled only by law enforcement after the fact, so a crypto platform that merely moves the funds onward has no reason to file any internal suspicious-activity report
options.why: Platforms still owe suspicious-activity reporting duties on fraud proceeds.

options.text: Fraud losses are the victim's responsibility alone, so the receiving platform faces no laundering exposure
options.why: The receiving platform can be laundering the proceeds regardless of victim fault.

options.text: Fraud proceeds stop being criminal property the moment they are converted between different cryptoassets
options.why: Conversion does not cleanse criminal property; it remains within scope.

spec.prompt: A customer routes trading gains through hidden wallets to avoid declaring them. Why is this an AML concern and not purely a tax matter?

options.text: Tax evasion is a predicate offence, so the undeclared proceeds are criminal property that can be laundered
options.why: Where tax evasion is a predicate, its proceeds fall within AML scope.

options.text: Tax matters are always outside AML scope because a separate authority collects the revenue involved
options.why: Predicate tax offences bring proceeds squarely into AML scope.

options.text: Only cash-based evasion counts, so gains held or moved as cryptoassets can never be a predicate offence
options.why: The form of the asset does not exclude crypto proceeds from being predicate proceeds.

options.text: Tax evasion becomes reportable only after a court has already convicted the customer of the underlying offence
options.why: Suspicion, not a prior conviction, is what triggers reporting duties.

spec.scenario: A customer onboards using a chain of entities and wallets spread across several jurisdictions.
spec.prompt: Which features suggest offshore structuring to conceal taxable crypto activity rather than legitimate arrangements?

items.label: Beneficial ownership is buried behind shell entities in secrecy jurisdictions with no clear business purpose
items.why: Opaque shell layering with no purpose is a structuring red flag.

items.label: Trading profits are routed to a nominee-held wallet that the customer directs but does not appear to own
items.why: Nominee control that hides ownership is a concealment indicator.

items.label: The customer files consistent tax residency information matching their verified documents
items.why: Consistent, verifiable residency information is not suspicious.

items.label: Value is deliberately fragmented across jurisdictions to stay below any single reporting trigger
items.why: Fragmenting to defeat reporting thresholds is structuring.

items.label: A genuine operating company holds crypto on a published, audited balance sheet
items.why: Transparent corporate holding is legitimate treasury activity.

spec.prompt: Match each concept to what it does in closing the crypto tax gap.

pairs.right: Require intermediaries to report customer crypto activity to tax authorities
pairs.why: Reporting rules make previously hidden activity visible to revenue authorities.

pairs.right: Uses cross-border entities and wallets to hide who ultimately controls the value
pairs.why: Structuring is the concealment method these frameworks aim to defeat.

pairs.right: Identifies the natural person who actually controls a structure
pairs.why: Ownership transparency pierces nominee and shell arrangements.

pairs.right: Shares reported account data between jurisdictions on a periodic basis
pairs.why: Cross-border data sharing reduces the value of hiding assets abroad.

spec.prompt: Order the stages of a typical ransomware extortion payment and its aftermath.

steps.label: Attackers encrypt a victim's systems and demand payment in crypto to a specified address
steps.why: The demand stage sets the extortion in motion.

steps.label: The victim, often via an intermediary, sends the ransom to the attacker-controlled address
steps.why: Payment transfers value to the attacker and creates sanctions exposure risk.

steps.label: The attackers layer the funds through mixers and multiple wallets to obscure the trail
steps.why: Layering hides the proceeds before cash-out.

steps.label: The proceeds are cashed out through a high-risk or sanctioned exchange with weak controls
steps.why: Complicit off-ramps convert the laundered ransom into usable value.

spec.prompt: A client asks your firm to help transmit a ransomware payment quickly. Beyond the extortion itself, what is the key AFC risk to weigh?

options.text: The payment may go to a sanctioned party, creating sanctions-breach and facilitation exposure regardless of urgency
options.why: Ransom recipients may be sanctioned, so paying risks a sanctions violation.

options.text: There is no risk at all provided the client is the genuine victim of the attack
options.why: Victim status does not remove sanctions and facilitation exposure.

options.text: The only concern is whether the network fee is high enough for the transfer to confirm quickly
options.why: Fee sizing is trivial next to sanctions and reporting risk.

options.text: Sanctions rules never apply to a ransomware payment, because the payer is acting entirely under duress at the time and that duress removes any strict-liability sanctions exposure
options.why: Duress does not automatically exempt a payment from sanctions rules.

title: Classify the illicit venue or actor

spec.prompt: Sort each element into the category it best fits within the illicit-marketplace and ransomware landscape.

items.label: A hidden-service site selling illegal goods for crypto with escrow between buyers and vendors
items.why: An anonymized marketplace with crypto escrow is a darknet market.

items.label: An exchange repeatedly processing proceeds for known criminal actors despite designation
items.why: A designated exchange knowingly cashing out proceeds is a complicit off-ramp.

items.label: A group deploying encryption malware and demanding crypto to restore access
items.why: Encrypt-and-extort for crypto defines a ransomware operation.

items.label: A vendor storefront accepting crypto for stolen data behind an anonymity network
items.why: Anonymous storefronts selling illicit data are darknet-market activity.

items.label: A nested service inside a larger exchange used to obscure the origin of tainted funds
items.why: Nested cash-out services function as complicit laundering off-ramps.

title: Match the typology to its mechanic

spec.prompt: Match each crypto laundering typology to how it actually works.

pairs.right: Small amounts are peeled off across a long sequence of hops while a remainder moves on
pairs.why: Peel chains shed value gradually to obscure the main flow.

pairs.right: A smaller service operates inside a larger exchange to piggyback on its access
pairs.why: Nesting hides illicit flows within a host exchange's activity.

pairs.right: Value is broken into many sub-threshold amounts across accounts or wallets
pairs.why: Structuring defeats reporting thresholds through fragmentation.

pairs.right: Value is swapped across different blockchains to break the on-chain trail
pairs.why: Moving between chains frustrates single-ledger tracing.

spec.scenario: An analyst reviews deposit and withdrawal patterns across a set of linked customer accounts.
spec.prompt: Which patterns are genuine structuring or smurfing red flags?

items.label: Many deposits sit just below a reporting threshold and cluster tightly in time
items.why: Repeated just-under-threshold amounts is the structuring signature.

items.label: Multiple accounts controlled by apparent proxies send equal amounts to one gathering wallet
items.why: Coordinated equal transfers from proxies into one wallet is smurfing.

items.label: A single customer makes one large, fully documented transfer for a stated business purpose
items.why: A transparent, well-documented large transfer is not structuring.

items.label: A customer suddenly fragments a known large balance into dozens of smaller outbound sends
items.why: Deliberate fragmentation of a large balance points to structuring.

items.label: A customer's monthly deposit varies naturally with their disclosed salary payments
items.why: Ordinary income variation is expected, not a red flag.

spec.prompt: A wallet receives a large deposit, then peels off small amounts through a long hop sequence before the remainder reaches a nested service. What is the best analyst conclusion?

options.text: The combined peel-chain and nesting pattern warrants escalation and investigation as likely layering
options.why: Peel chains feeding a nested service is a strong layering indicator that should be escalated.

options.text: No action is needed because each individual small transfer sits below a reporting threshold on its own
options.why: Individual sub-threshold amounts do not neutralize an overall structuring and layering pattern.

options.text: The activity is automatically clean because it all occurred on a single transparent public blockchain
options.why: Transparency of the ledger does not make an obfuscation pattern legitimate.

options.text: It can be dismissed as ordinary trading since the wallet paid standard network fees on every hop
options.why: Normal fees say nothing about the legitimacy of the underlying flow.

title: Is publishing code a VASP activity?

spec.prompt: A software developer publishes open-source code for a peer-to-peer trading protocol. They host no service, hold no customer funds, control no keys, and conduct no transfers for anyone. How should this be characterized under FATF Recommendation 15 and INR.15?

options.text: The developer is not a VASP for publishing code alone, since they do not conduct covered VASP activities as a business for or on behalf of others.
options.why: The VASP definition turns on conducting covered activities for others as a business, not on merely writing software.

options.text: The developer is automatically a VASP, because any software that touches virtual assets falls within the VASP definition.
options.why: There is no rule that any software touching virtual assets is a VASP; the covered activities test still applies.

options.text: The developer is a VASP because writing and publishing code that other people later choose to use to transfer value is itself the covered VASP activity of transferring virtual assets on behalf of others.
options.why: Publishing code is not the same as transferring virtual assets for others; the developer performs no transfer for a customer.

options.text: The developer is permanently exempt from all AML obligations, so no future facts could ever bring them within scope.
options.why: Scope depends on conduct, so later activity such as running a hosted service could bring them in.

title: In scope as a VASP activity?

spec.prompt: Sort each activity by whether it is a covered VASP activity under the FATF definition.

items.label: A business exchanges customers' fiat currency for virtual assets.
items.why: Exchange between virtual assets and fiat currency is a covered VASP activity.

items.label: A business safekeeps virtual assets and holds the private keys on behalf of its customers.
items.why: Safekeeping or administration of assets or instruments enabling control is a covered VASP activity.

items.label: An individual buys crypto with their own money and holds it long term for themselves.
items.why: Acting for oneself, not as a business for others, is outside the VASP definition.

items.label: A business transfers virtual assets between wallets on behalf of its customers.
items.why: Transfer of virtual assets on behalf of others is a covered VASP activity.

items.label: A merchant accepts virtual assets as payment for goods but does not exchange or transfer them for others.
items.why: Simply accepting an asset as payment is not itself a covered VASP activity.

spec.prompt: Match each Recommendation 15 / INR.15 obligation to its correct description.

pairs.right: VASPs must be licensed or registered in the jurisdiction where they are created or established.
pairs.why: INR.15 requires VASPs to be licensed or registered, not merely self-declared.

pairs.right: A competent authority monitors and supervises VASPs for compliance, not a self-regulation-only model.
pairs.why: VASPs must be subject to effective systems for supervision by a competent authority.

pairs.right: VASPs apply customer due diligence, recordkeeping, and suspicious transaction reporting.
pairs.why: VASPs are subject to the same preventive measures as other reporting institutions.

pairs.right: Countries and VASPs identify, assess, and mitigate the ML/TF risks of virtual asset activities.
pairs.why: R.15 requires understanding and mitigating the risks of virtual asset activities.

title: Order a compliant Travel Rule transfer

spec.prompt: Put the steps of a compliant Travel Rule transfer between two VASPs in the correct order.

steps.label: The originating VASP collects and verifies the required originator information and the required beneficiary information.
steps.why: The originating VASP must obtain and hold the required data before the transfer.

steps.label: The originating VASP transmits the required data set securely to the beneficiary VASP, at or before the transfer.
steps.why: The data must travel immediately and securely with the transfer.

steps.label: The beneficiary VASP receives the data and screens the parties and information for sanctions and suspicious indicators.
steps.why: The beneficiary VASP must review the received information on receipt.

steps.label: The beneficiary VASP records the information and releases the funds or acts further if red flags arise.
steps.why: The beneficiary VASP must hold the data and take risk-based action.

title: Red flags in a received Travel Rule message

spec.scenario: Your VASP receives an incoming above-threshold transfer together with its accompanying originator and beneficiary data.
spec.prompt: Which of these are genuine Travel Rule red flags?

items.label: The originator name field is blank for an above-threshold transfer.
items.why: Missing required originator information is a core Travel Rule deficiency.

items.label: The message carries the originator name and wallet address but omits the required physical address or ID number.
items.why: Above-threshold transfers require the fuller originator data set, not just name and address.

items.label: The beneficiary name in the message does not match the account holder receiving the funds.
items.why: A beneficiary mismatch signals possible misdirection or false information.

items.label: The message includes a complete, verified originator data set that matches the transfer.
items.why: A complete and consistent data set is exactly what compliance expects.

items.label: The transfer settled in a widely used stablecoin rather than the network's native coin.
items.why: The asset type alone is not a Travel Rule red flag.

title: Match the Travel Rule duty to the party

spec.prompt: Match each Travel Rule element or duty to what it correctly describes.

pairs.left: Originator name and originator wallet address
pairs.right: Required originator information the originating VASP must obtain and hold.
pairs.why: These identify the sender and are part of the required originator data.

pairs.left: Beneficiary name and beneficiary wallet address
pairs.right: Required beneficiary information transmitted to the beneficiary VASP.
pairs.why: These identify the recipient and travel with the transfer.

pairs.left: Transmit the data set immediately and securely
pairs.right: Obligation of the originating VASP at the time of the transfer.
pairs.why: The originating VASP must submit the data securely when the transfer is made.

pairs.left: Obtain, hold, and screen the received information
pairs.right: Obligation of the beneficiary VASP on receipt.
pairs.why: The beneficiary VASP must keep and check the information it receives.

spec.prompt: Your VASP must send a cross-border transfer to a counterparty VASP in a jurisdiction that has not yet implemented the Travel Rule and cannot receive the required data set. What is the appropriate risk-based approach?

options.text: Still collect and retain the required originator and beneficiary information, apply a counterparty and transaction risk assessment, and decide whether to proceed, delay, or decline.
options.why: Your own obligation to obtain and hold data persists, and residual risk is managed case by case.

options.text: Proceed automatically with no data collection at all, because the receiving jurisdiction has not implemented the rule, and that gap completely removes your own obligation to obtain and hold originator information.
options.why: The counterparty's gap does not erase your own collection and retention duties.

options.text: Refuse every transfer to any jurisdiction that has not yet implemented the Travel Rule, without any risk assessment.
options.why: A blanket refusal ignores the required risk-based approach.

options.text: Send the funds and email the data to any address you find, without verifying it belongs to the counterparty.
options.why: Transmitting data to an unverified recipient creates its own compliance and security risk.

spec.prompt: Sort each outgoing transfer by whether it goes to a counterparty VASP or to an unhosted wallet.

items.label: Transfer to a customer account held at another regulated exchange.
items.why: The receiving account sits at a VASP, so counterparty VASP due diligence applies.

items.label: Transfer to a self-hosted wallet controlled directly by your own customer, with no institution involved.
items.why: No intermediary VASP is present, so risk-based unhosted-wallet measures apply.

items.label: Transfer to a hosted account at a custodian that holds keys for its users.
items.why: A custodian holding keys for users is a VASP counterparty.

items.label: Transfer to a wallet an individual controls with their own private keys, not through any service.
items.why: A wallet controlled directly by a person with no service is unhosted.

items.label: Transfer to a beneficiary account at an overseas exchange identified through its Travel Rule participation.
items.why: The overseas exchange is a counterparty VASP that exchanges Travel Rule data.

title: Counterparty VASP due diligence red flags

spec.scenario: You are performing due diligence on a counterparty VASP before enabling transfers to it.
spec.prompt: Which findings are genuine counterparty due diligence red flags?

items.label: The counterparty cannot demonstrate any license or registration in the jurisdiction where it operates.
items.why: An unlicensed or unregistered counterparty is a significant risk indicator.

items.label: The counterparty advertises that it performs no customer identification and permits fully anonymous accounts.
items.why: Absence of customer identification signals weak or missing AML controls.

items.label: The counterparty is unwilling to exchange required Travel Rule information or to name its regulator.
items.why: Refusing to share required data or identify a regulator undermines counterparty trust.

items.label: The counterparty uses a different Travel Rule messaging protocol but supports an interoperability bridge.
items.why: Protocol differences are a solvable interoperability matter, not a control failure.

items.label: The counterparty is incorporated in a different country from yours.
items.why: Cross-border operation alone is not a red flag.

title: A hit on a designated address

spec.prompt: Your screening flags that an incoming transfer originates from a wallet address that appears on the sanctions authority's designated (SDN-type) list. Under a strict-liability sanctions regime, what must you do?

options.text: Block or reject the transaction consistent with your obligations and report the blocked property to the relevant authority, rather than processing it.
options.why: Dealings tied to a designated address must be blocked or rejected and reported.

options.text: Process the transfer normally, because the customer receiving the funds is not themselves a designated person, so the designated origin address does not create any obligation for you.
options.why: The designated origin triggers obligations regardless of whether your customer is listed.

options.text: Return the funds quietly to the originating address with no report, so the customer is not inconvenienced.
options.why: Returning funds to a sanctioned source without reporting defeats the sanctions obligation.

options.text: Ignore the alert, because sanctions rules apply only to fiat currency and never to virtual assets.
options.why: Sanctions apply to virtual assets the same as to fiat value.

title: Sanctions red flags in on-chain activity

spec.scenario: You review a customer's on-chain activity for sanctions exposure.
spec.prompt: Which observations are genuine sanctions red flags?

items.label: A deposit received directly from an address on the sanctions authority's designated list.
items.why: A direct link to a designated address is a clear sanctions exposure.

items.label: Value routed through a mixer immediately before reaching your platform, apparently to obscure a designated-address origin.
items.why: Deliberate obfuscation of a designated origin is a strong sanctions-evasion signal.

items.label: Wallet and access patterns indicating the customer is transacting from a comprehensively sanctioned jurisdiction.
items.why: Activity from a comprehensively sanctioned jurisdiction raises direct sanctions risk.

items.label: The customer occasionally moves assets to a hardware wallet for self-custody between trades.
items.why: Ordinary self-custody is not a sanctions indicator by itself.

items.label: The customer's transfers settle in a major stablecoin widely used across the market.
items.why: Using a common stablecoin is not a sanctions red flag.

title: Match the sanctions concept to its meaning

spec.prompt: Match each crypto sanctions concept to its correct meaning.

pairs.right: Violations can arise without intent or knowledge, so screening controls must be robust.
pairs.why: Strict-liability regimes do not require intent, raising the bar for controls.

pairs.right: A wallet address associated with a sanctioned person or entity that must be blocked or rejected.
pairs.why: Designated addresses carry the same restrictions as the listed party.

pairs.right: Freezing the property and reporting it, rather than returning the funds to the sender.
pairs.why: Blocking holds the property and reports it, unlike a return or rejection.

pairs.right: Checking wallet addresses in transactions against current sanctions lists before processing.
pairs.why: Screening addresses before processing prevents prohibited dealings.

spec.prompt: Two crypto businesses ask whether FinCEN's rules make them money transmitters. One runs an online platform that exchanges customers' bitcoin for US dollars and back. The other is a software company that buys crypto only for its own treasury and never handles customer funds. Under FinCEN's 2013 convertible-virtual-currency guidance, which is right?

options.text: The exchange platform is a money transmitter and must register with FinCEN; the software firm, using crypto only for itself, is a user and is not.
options.why: An exchanger transmits value for others; a firm using crypto for itself is a user.

options.text: Both are money transmitters, because any business that so much as touches convertible virtual currency falls inside FinCEN's money-services-business rules, whatever it actually does with the funds.
options.why: Only transmitting or exchanging value for others triggers the rule, not any contact with crypto.

options.text: Neither is regulated, because FinCEN's 2013 guidance was only interpretive and reaches banks alone.
options.why: The 2013 guidance applied money-services-business rules to crypto exchangers, not only banks.

options.text: Only the software firm is a money transmitter, since holding crypto on its own balance sheet is the regulated act.
options.why: Holding crypto on your own balance sheet is a user act, not transmission.

spec.prompt: The US regime is layers, each run by a different authority. Match each question to the authority whose layer answers it.

pairs.left: Whether a token is a security subject to registration and disclosure
pairs.right: The SEC, which regulates assets that are securities
pairs.why: Securities fall to the SEC.

pairs.left: Crypto commodity derivatives such as futures
pairs.right: The CFTC, which also reaches the spot market through anti-fraud and anti-manipulation powers
pairs.why: Commodity derivatives, and spot-market fraud, fall to the CFTC.

pairs.left: AML duties of a business exchanging crypto for customers
pairs.right: FinCEN, under the Bank Secrecy Act money-transmitter rules
pairs.why: AML for exchangers is FinCEN's layer.

pairs.left: Virtual-currency business activity involving a New York resident
pairs.right: New York's Department of Financial Services, through the BitLicense
pairs.why: New York draws its own perimeter with the BitLicense.

title: Exposed to FinCEN and the DOJ?

spec.scenario: A crypto exchange operates from abroad but actively markets to and serves US customers, including residents of New York. A reviewer checks its compliance posture.
spec.prompt: Which of these are genuine red flags that the business is exposed to FinCEN civil penalties or a DOJ criminal case?

items.label: It exchanges crypto for US customers but has never registered with FinCEN as a money services business.
items.why: Exchanging for US customers without FinCEN registration is unlawful transmission.

items.label: It has no written anti-money-laundering program and files no suspicious activity reports.
items.why: No AML program and no reports breach core Bank Secrecy Act duties.

items.label: It serves New York residents' virtual-currency business activity but holds no BitLicense.
items.why: Serving New York residents' virtual-currency activity needs a BitLicense.

items.label: It publishes audited financial statements and lists its corporate officers on its website.
items.why: Publishing accounts and naming officers is transparency, not a violation.

items.label: It converts part of its own corporate profits into bitcoin as a treasury reserve.
items.why: Holding crypto for its own treasury is a user act, not transmission.

spec.prompt: The EU keeps two strands separate: MiCA for market and prudential rules, and the AML framework for financial crime. Sort each obligation into the strand it belongs to.

items.label: Authorising a firm to provide crypto services and passport them across the EU
items.why: Authorising and passporting CASPs is MiCA's market-access job.

items.label: The rules on how a stablecoin is issued, backed by reserves, and disclosed
items.why: Stablecoin issuance and reserves are MiCA rules.

items.label: Conduct and disclosure rules for public offers of crypto-assets
items.why: Public-offer conduct and disclosure sit in MiCA.

items.label: Requiring originator and beneficiary information to travel with a crypto transfer
items.why: The Travel Rule sits in the Transfer of Funds Regulation, the AML strand.

items.label: Customer due diligence and suspicious-activity reporting by obliged entities
items.why: CDD and reporting are AML-package obligations, not MiCA.

spec.prompt: A crypto-asset service provider is authorised as a CASP by the national competent authority in one EU member state and now wants to serve customers in several other member states. What does MiCA let it do?

options.text: Passport its services across the whole EU on that single authorisation, without a separate licence in each member state.
options.why: A single authorisation passports across the whole EU.

options.text: It must apply again for a fresh standalone authorisation from the competent authority of every member state where it wants even one customer, because a MiCA authorisation is valid only inside the issuing country.
options.why: MiCA is a Regulation, so one authorisation is EU-wide, not per country.

options.text: It may serve other member states only after each national parliament transposes MiCA into its own domestic statute.
options.why: A Regulation is directly applicable and needs no national transposition.

options.text: It gains market access everywhere but is exempt from the EU Travel Rule on cross-border transfers.
options.why: Market access does not exempt a CASP from the EU Travel Rule.

spec.prompt: Put these EU crypto-regime milestones in the order they take effect.

steps.label: MiCA's stablecoin rules take effect, regulating how asset-referenced and e-money tokens are issued and backed.
steps.why: The stablecoin strand of MiCA applied first.

steps.label: MiCA's CASP authorisation and broader market rules take effect.
steps.why: CASP authorisation followed the stablecoin rules.

steps.label: The AML Regulation single rulebook takes effect, harmonising customer due diligence and reporting across the bloc.
steps.why: The directly applicable AML rulebook applies later.

steps.label: AMLA begins directly supervising selected high-risk cross-border financial-sector firms.
steps.why: EU-level direct supervision begins last.

spec.prompt: The UK regime fits together as a gate, a rulebook, and the law behind them, plus one separate regime. Match each part to its role.

pairs.right: The gate: a firm in scope cannot lawfully carry on cryptoasset business in the UK without it
pairs.why: Registration is the perimeter's gate.

pairs.right: The rulebook: once registered, a firm runs due diligence, monitoring, records, and the Travel Rule
pairs.why: The regulations are the operating rulebook.

pairs.right: The law behind it: it defines the money-laundering offences and channels suspicions through the SAR and consent regime
pairs.why: POCA is the criminal law and reporting channel.

pairs.right: A separate regime protecting consumers from misleading crypto marketing, not about money laundering
pairs.why: Promotions rules are consumer protection, not AML.

spec.scenario: A reviewer looks at several crypto businesses active around the UK. FCA registration is the gate, and carrying on in-scope business without it is a criminal offence.
spec.prompt: Which of these are genuine red flags of an unlawful, unregistered posture under the Money Laundering Regulations?

items.label: It operates cryptoasset ATMs in the UK exchanging cash for crypto, but has never registered with the FCA.
items.why: A crypto ATM is exchange activity, and unregistered operation is a criminal offence.

items.label: It safeguards UK customers' cryptoassets and the private keys to them as a business, without FCA registration.
items.why: Safeguarding customers' crypto or keys is custodian activity that must be registered.

items.label: It carries on cryptoasset exchange as a business in the UK after its FCA application was refused.
items.why: Carrying on the business after a refused application is unlawful.

items.label: It holds FCA registration for AML supervision and has not sought a separate full market authorisation.
items.why: FCA registration is AML supervision, not a full market authorisation, so this is correct.

items.label: It has one UK-resident customer but conducts all its exchange activity, staff, and systems entirely outside the UK.
items.why: Merely having a UK customer, with all activity abroad, may not be carrying on business in the UK.

spec.prompt: A person converts the proceeds of a fraud into bitcoin abroad, brings the value to the UK in self-hosted wallets, and a UK-based associate helps convert it into property. Under UK law, what is the position?

options.text: Dealing with criminal property to conceal or convert it is money laundering under the Proceeds of Crime Act, so the associate commits an offence and the value can be seized.
options.why: Dealing with criminal property to conceal or convert it is POCA money laundering.

options.text: Because the underlying fraud happened abroad and the value sits in self-hosted wallets rather than a bank account, the Proceeds of Crime Act cannot reach it and no money-laundering offence arises in the UK at all.
options.why: POCA reaches criminal property in crypto form, wherever the value sits.

options.text: It is only a breach of the Money Laundering Regulations, handled by an FCA fine rather than a criminal prosecution.
options.why: This is a POCA criminal matter, not merely a supervisory breach.

options.text: No offence arises until the crypto is converted back into pounds through a registered exchange.
options.why: The offence does not wait for conversion back into pounds.

spec.prompt: You must locate the crypto obligations of a jurisdiction you have never studied. Put the steps of the mapping method in order.

steps.label: Recognise that the jurisdiction implements the shared FATF baseline, so its core obligations will be familiar.
steps.why: Start from the baseline every regime implements.

steps.label: Go to the primary law and find the licensing or registration regime and the supervisor.
steps.why: Locate the gate and the authority first.

steps.label: Read how that law defines a VASP and which activities fall inside the perimeter.
steps.why: Scope is where regimes differ most, so pin it down.

steps.label: Locate the Travel Rule, the reporting channel to the financial intelligence unit, and the sanctions duties.
steps.why: Finish with the specific preventive obligations.

spec.prompt: When you map a new jurisdiction, some things are broadly the same everywhere because they come from the FATF baseline, and some are the points where regimes genuinely differ. Sort each item.

items.label: That VASPs must be licensed or registered and supervised for AML
items.why: Recommendation 15 and INR.15 set this everywhere.

items.label: That customer due diligence and suspicious-transaction reporting to the FIU are required
items.why: These preventive measures are part of the baseline.

items.label: Exactly which originator and beneficiary fields must travel, and whether a low-value exemption applies
items.why: The Travel Rule threshold and data set are set locally.

items.label: Whether in-scope activity reaches decentralised-finance arrangements
items.why: Regimes draw the VASP and DeFi line differently.

items.label: Whether market and prudential regulation is bundled with AML or kept separate
items.why: The three regimes each answered this differently.

spec.prompt: Mapping an unfamiliar jurisdiction, you find a business that, for its customers, exchanges one virtual asset for another and safekeeps their virtual assets. A colleague says to check the recommendations written for banks. Where does the FATF baseline actually place this business?

options.text: It is a VASP, defined by activity, and its regulation sits under Recommendation 15 and INR.15, not the recommendations written for banks.
options.why: VASPs sit under Recommendation 15 and INR.15, defined by activity.

options.text: Because it handles customer assets much like a deposit-taking institution, its obligations are found in the recommendations for banks, and the virtual-asset recommendation applies only to firms that issue their own new tokens.
options.why: VASP regulation is not the bank recommendations, and it covers exchange and custody, not only issuance.

options.text: It falls outside FATF entirely, because exchanging one virtual asset for another is not a regulated activity.
options.why: Exchanging one virtual asset for another is squarely in the VASP activity list.

options.text: It is a VASP only if it also exchanges virtual assets for money; crypto-to-crypto exchange alone is out of scope.
options.why: The FATF activity list includes crypto-to-crypto exchange, not only crypto-to-fiat.

spec.prompt: A crypto exchange rates the inherent ML/TF risk of its privacy-coin trading pair as high. After adding blockchain analytics screening, enhanced monitoring, and withdrawal limits, its assessment shows the residual risk as medium. What does this movement from high to medium tell you?

options.text: The controls are reducing the risk that remains after mitigation, but the underlying activity is still inherently high risk.
options.why: Residual risk is the inherent risk left over once controls are applied.

options.text: The inherent risk has been permanently lowered to medium, so the privacy-coin pair can now be treated as a standard low-attention product with no special handling.
options.why: Controls change the residual score, not the inherent risk of the activity itself.

options.text: Residual risk being medium means no further controls are ever needed here.
options.why: Residual risk still needs monitoring and may exceed the firm's appetite.

options.text: The medium score means the analytics tool failed and the risk was overstated.
options.why: A lower residual score reflects controls working, not a tool failure.

spec.scenario: You are scoping the inherent ML/TF risk of a new retail crypto brokerage before any controls are designed.
spec.prompt: Which of these product and customer characteristics raise the inherent risk that belongs in the risk assessment?

items.label: The platform will support deposits and withdrawals to and from self-hosted wallets with no counterparty attribution.
items.why: Unattributed self-hosted transfers raise laundering exposure.

items.label: A significant share of target customers are based in jurisdictions with weak or absent VASP supervision.
items.why: Weak-supervision geographies elevate inherent risk.

items.label: The product roadmap includes support for anonymity-enhanced privacy coins.
items.why: Privacy coins obscure the transaction trail.

items.label: The firm plans to require government-ID and liveness verification for every account at onboarding.
items.why: That is a control that lowers residual risk, not an inherent risk driver.

items.label: The firm will publish an annual transparency report about law-enforcement requests.
items.why: Disclosure practices do not raise inherent ML/TF risk.

spec.prompt: Sort each factor into the risk-assessment dimension it primarily belongs to.

items.label: A high proportion of accounts held by politically exposed persons
items.why: Customer type is a customer-dimension factor.

items.label: Offering an anonymous crypto prepaid card that spends directly from wallet balances
items.why: The feature is a product and service characteristic.

items.label: Serving customers resident in a FATF grey-listed jurisdiction
items.why: The jurisdiction of the customer base is geographic.

items.label: Adding support for instant cross-chain bridge swaps inside the app
items.why: A bridging feature is a product-dimension factor.

items.label: Onboarding legal-entity customers with opaque, multi-layered ownership structures
items.why: Ownership opacity is a customer characteristic.

spec.prompt: Match each onboarding technique to what it is designed to establish.

pairs.left: Asking the customer to sign a message from the wallet's private key
pairs.right: Proof that the customer controls the wallet
pairs.why: A valid signature proves control of the key.

pairs.left: A small verification transfer from the customer's stated wallet
pairs.right: Confirmation that the depositing wallet is the customer's
pairs.why: The transfer links the wallet to the account.

pairs.left: A liveness and document check at sign-up
pairs.right: Confirmation the person is genuine and present
pairs.why: Liveness defeats non-face-to-face impersonation.

pairs.left: Running the deposit address through blockchain analytics
pairs.right: Assessment of the address's exposure to illicit funds
pairs.why: Analytics reveal exposure to risky clusters.

spec.prompt: Order the steps for onboarding a high-net-worth customer who intends to fund the account from a large self-hosted wallet balance.

steps.label: Verify the customer's identity with a document and liveness check
steps.why: Identity must be established first.

steps.label: Establish that the customer controls the funding wallet via a signed message or verification transfer
steps.why: Control links the wallet to the verified customer.

steps.label: Screen the wallet and deposit address for exposure using blockchain analytics
steps.why: Exposure screening flags illicit-linked funds.

steps.label: Test the stated source of funds against the wallet's on-chain history and document the conclusion
steps.why: Source of funds must be evidenced to a documented standard.

spec.prompt: A new customer wants to deposit a large crypto balance and explains their wealth by saying, 'I bought very early and just held.' How should you handle source of funds for this on-chain wealth?

options.text: Test the claim against the wallet's on-chain history, seeking evidence consistent with early acquisition, and document the conclusion to a reasonable standard.
options.why: On-chain wealth can be evidenced against the public ledger.

options.text: Accept the explanation as given, because self-custodied crypto wealth predates any exchange records and therefore cannot be verified by any means available to the firm at all.
options.why: The blockchain itself provides a verifiable transaction history.

options.text: Reject the customer outright, since early holdings are always a laundering indicator.
options.why: Early acquisition is not itself an illicit signal.

options.text: Ask only for a bank statement showing the original fiat purchase years ago.
options.why: A single stale document is not sufficient testing.

spec.scenario: You are reviewing a retail customer's risk rating during a periodic refresh.
spec.prompt: Which findings should push this customer's risk rating higher?

items.label: Recent deposits show direct exposure to a wallet cluster attributed to a darknet market
items.why: Direct illicit exposure elevates risk.

items.label: The customer recently moved residence to a jurisdiction under FATF increased monitoring
items.why: Higher-risk geography raises the rating.

items.label: Withdrawal patterns show repeated transfers to a known mixing service
items.why: Mixer use is an obfuscation signal.

items.label: The customer logs in from the same device and verified phone each month
items.why: Stable, verified access is not a risk elevator.

items.label: The customer's trading volume rose modestly in line with a documented salary increase
items.why: Explained, proportionate growth is not a flag.

spec.prompt: Sort each on-chain exposure finding by whether it is direct or indirect exposure.

items.label: A deposit received straight from an address on a sanctions list
items.why: The counterparty is the illicit entity itself.

items.label: Funds that reached the customer after passing through three intermediary wallets from a darknet cluster
items.why: Intervening hops make the exposure indirect.

items.label: A withdrawal sent directly to a mixing service's deposit address
items.why: The counterparty is the illicit service directly.

items.label: Incoming value that is two hops removed from a ransomware-linked wallet
items.why: Separation by hops is indirect exposure.

items.label: Value received from an intermediary wallet that was itself funded by a scam-linked address
items.why: The direct counterparty is an intermediary, so exposure is indirect.

spec.prompt: A customer rated low risk at onboarding just received a deposit that blockchain analytics place two hops from a sanctioned cluster. Your model re-rates on events, not only on a calendar. What is the right response?

options.text: Adjust the rating to reflect the weighted indirect exposure and trigger the monitoring consequences that the higher band now requires.
options.why: Event-driven re-rating updates the score immediately.

options.text: Leave the rating unchanged until the customer's next scheduled annual review, because a single deposit is never enough to alter a risk score that was set at onboarding.
options.why: Dynamic scoring re-rates on events, not by the calendar.

options.text: Immediately close the account, since any sanctioned-cluster proximity is a direct hit.
options.why: Indirect exposure is weighted, not an automatic block.

options.text: Treat the exposure as direct and file as if the customer transacted with the sanctioned entity.
options.why: Two hops is indirect, not a direct counterparty.

title: Can the offshore flag escape the rules

spec.prompt: An exchange incorporated in an offshore jurisdiction with no crypto licensing regime actively serves retail customers in the US and the EU and carries on cryptoasset business in the UK. Its counsel argues that only its home jurisdiction's rules apply. Is that correct?

options.text: No. Obligations attach where the activity is carried on, so it can trigger US registration, EU authorisation, and UK registration despite the offshore incorporation.
options.why: The perimeter follows the activity and its nexus, not the flag of incorporation.

options.text: Yes, because a firm is only ever subject to the financial-crime rules of the single jurisdiction in which it is legally incorporated, and choosing a light-touch home base lawfully removes all other obligations.
options.why: Incorporating offshore does not escape the perimeter where business is carried on.

options.text: Yes, provided the exchange adds a disclaimer that it does not intend to serve those markets.
options.why: A disclaimer does not override actual activity in a market.

options.text: No, but only the US rules can ever apply beyond the home jurisdiction.
options.why: The EU and UK regimes also reach this activity.

spec.prompt: Match each jurisdiction or standard to the gate a crypto business must pass to operate there.

pairs.right: FinCEN MSB registration plus state money-transmitter licences where required
pairs.why: The US layers federal registration and state licensing.

pairs.right: MiCA CASP authorisation, with AML and Travel Rule duties coming separately from the EU AML framework
pairs.why: MiCA is market authorisation; AML duties sit in the AML framework.

pairs.right: FCA registration for carrying on cryptoasset business in the UK
pairs.why: The UK trigger is a facts-based business test.

pairs.right: The Recommendation 15 and INR.15 VASP perimeter that jurisdictions implement
pairs.why: FATF sets the standard others transpose into law.

spec.prompt: Order the steps a new crypto business should follow to scope its regulatory perimeter before launch.

steps.label: Identify the specific activities you will perform and whether they are regulated VASP or CASP activities
steps.why: Scope starts with what you actually do.
