steps.label: Determine every jurisdiction where you have a nexus through establishment or the customers you serve
steps.why: Nexus determines which regimes reach you.

steps.label: Map the registration or authorisation gate each of those jurisdictions imposes
steps.why: Each jurisdiction has its own gate.

steps.label: Obtain the required authorisations and stand up the AML obligations before you begin operating
steps.why: Authorisation must precede operating.

spec.prompt: A crypto exchange's deposit-monitoring scenario fires an alert whenever a customer receives value with any indirect exposure to a high-risk cluster. Analysts close about 95 percent of these alerts as no concern, and the backlog is growing. What is the most defensible response?

options.text: Raise the exposure threshold so only direct or one-hop exposure to the cluster triggers an alert, then validate the change against known true positives before deploying it.
options.why: Risk-based tuning plus validation preserves detection while cutting noise, which is sound model risk discipline.

options.text: Turn the scenario off entirely until the backlog clears, since the alerts are almost all false positives anyway.
options.why: Disabling a control creates a coverage gap and removes detection for genuine exposure.

options.text: Instruct analysts to automatically close every indirect-exposure alert without any review, so the backlog clears quickly and the team can move on to other queues across the business.
options.why: Blanket auto-closing without review means real suspicious activity is dispositioned as clean.

options.text: Lower the exposure threshold further so even more alerts fire, on the theory that broader coverage is always safer for the program.
options.why: More noise worsens the backlog and buries true positives rather than improving detection.

title: First two weeks of a new account

spec.scenario: A newly onboarded retail customer's account shows the following activity in its first two weeks.
spec.prompt: Which items are genuine monitoring red flags?

items.label: Deposits arrive from a self-hosted wallet and are immediately withdrawn to a different self-hosted wallet, repeated several times with no trading in between.
items.why: Rapid in-and-out pass-through with no economic purpose is classic layering behaviour.

items.label: Incoming deposits trace back within two hops to an address cluster attributed to a darknet marketplace.
items.why: Close on-chain exposure to illicit clusters is a direct monitoring concern.

items.label: The customer completed identity verification and enabled two-factor authentication at signup.
items.why: These are normal, expected onboarding security steps, not indicators of risk.

items.label: Deposit amounts are consistently set just below the exchange's enhanced-review threshold.
items.why: Amounts hugging a threshold suggest deliberate structuring to avoid scrutiny.

items.label: Some deposits arrive from a well-known regulated exchange where the customer also holds a verified account.
items.why: Transfers between a customer's own regulated accounts are ordinary and low concern by themselves.

spec.prompt: Sort each monitoring-assurance activity into the type of testing it represents.

items.label: Reviewing a sample of alerts the system generated to confirm they were correctly escalated or closed.
items.why: Above-the-line testing examines the alerts that actually fired.

items.label: Sampling transactions that fell just under a scenario threshold to check whether any should have alerted.
items.why: Below-the-line testing probes activity that did not alert for missed coverage.

items.label: Testing whether a lowered threshold would have caught known laundering typologies that produced no alert.
items.why: Checking for undetected typologies looks below the current alerting line.

items.label: Auditing closed alerts to confirm analysts documented a sound disposition rationale.
items.why: Assessing the quality of dispositioned alerts is above-the-line assurance.

items.label: Selecting customers with no alerts in a period and re-running their activity against updated detection logic.
items.why: Re-running non-alerting populations tests for gaps beneath the threshold.

title: Screening techniques and what they do

spec.prompt: Match each screening technique to what it does.

pairs.right: Flags near-identical names despite spelling variants, so a variant can still match a designated name.
pairs.why: Fuzzy matching tolerates minor spelling differences to avoid missed hits.

pairs.right: Checks a blockchain address against lists of designated wallet identifiers before releasing a transfer.
pairs.why: Address screening compares on-chain identifiers to sanctioned-wallet references.

pairs.right: Uses IP and device signals to detect access from a sanctioned jurisdiction.
pairs.why: Geolocation screening targets jurisdictional exposure rather than names.

pairs.right: Normalises names written in different scripts to a common form before comparison.
pairs.why: Transliteration handling aligns cross-script names so they can be compared.

spec.scenario: A compliance officer reviews a crypto firm's sanctions-screening configuration.
spec.prompt: Which items are genuine screening weaknesses?

items.label: Name screening performs exact-string matching only, so any spelling variant or transliteration passes through.
items.why: Exact-only matching misses variant spellings of designated names.

items.label: Wallet-address screening runs at onboarding only, not on each incoming or outgoing transfer.
items.why: Point-in-time address screening misses newly designated wallets in live flows.

items.label: The match threshold is set so tight that clear phonetic variants of designated names never generate a hit.
items.why: An over-tight threshold suppresses true matches and lowers recall.

items.label: The firm re-screens its full customer base within a day whenever a sanctions list is updated.
items.why: Prompt re-screening after list updates is sound practice, not a weakness.

items.label: Screening lists are refreshed automatically each day from the official designated-party sources.
items.why: Automated refresh from official sources is good list management.

spec.prompt: A crypto firm's name-screening engine is missing designated persons whose names appear with alternate spellings, but leadership also complains about false positives. Which adjustment best balances recall and precision?

options.text: Lower the match-score threshold to raise recall, then use secondary data such as date of birth to filter the extra hits.
options.why: Wider matching plus corroborating attributes recovers missed hits without flooding review with noise.

options.text: Raise the match-score threshold so fewer alerts fire, accepting that some designated persons with spelling variants will keep passing through screening.
options.why: Tightening the threshold worsens the recall gap and lets true matches escape.

options.text: Keep the current threshold exactly as it is and simply hire additional analysts to work through whichever alerts the existing configuration happens to produce for the team each day.
options.why: More staff cannot review alerts the engine never generated for the missed names.

options.text: Disable fuzzy matching and rely on exact matching only, since exact matching produces the fewest false positives for the review team.
options.why: Exact-only matching maximises missed variants, which is the failure being reported.

title: Handling an outbound transfer under the Travel Rule

spec.prompt: Put the operational steps for an outbound VASP transfer under the Travel Rule in the correct order.

steps.label: Determine whether the transfer meets or exceeds the applicable Travel Rule value threshold.
steps.why: The threshold determines whether Travel Rule data-sharing obligations apply at all.

steps.label: Identify the counterparty VASP that controls the beneficiary address.
steps.why: You must know who the receiving institution is before you can send data to it.

steps.label: Confirm the counterparty VASP can securely receive Travel Rule data and assess any sunrise gap.
steps.why: Checking readiness drives the sunrise decision on whether and how to proceed.

steps.label: Transmit the required originator and beneficiary information in a structured IVMS 101 message with the transfer.
steps.why: Sending interoperable data with the transfer completes the obligation.

title: Matching Travel Rule situations to handling

spec.prompt: Match each Travel Rule situation to the correct operational handling.

pairs.left: The beneficiary VASP is in a jurisdiction that has not yet implemented the Travel Rule.
pairs.right: Apply a risk-based sunrise policy, deciding whether to send data, hold, or proceed with mitigations.
pairs.why: The sunrise gap requires a documented risk-based decision, not automatic transmission.

pairs.left: The withdrawal is to a self-hosted wallet the customer controls.
pairs.right: Collect and, on a risk basis, verify the required customer information rather than exchange data with a counterparty VASP.
pairs.why: There is no receiving institution, so the duty shifts to customer-side data collection.

pairs.left: Originator and beneficiary data must be structured for interoperable transmission.
pairs.right: Encode the details in the IVMS 101 data standard.
pairs.why: IVMS 101 is the common format that lets VASPs exchange data reliably.

pairs.left: You cannot tell which VASP, if any, controls the destination address.
pairs.right: Attempt counterparty identification before releasing the transfer.
pairs.why: You must resolve the counterparty before you can meet the data-sharing duty.

spec.prompt: A customer requests a withdrawal above the Travel Rule threshold to an address your attribution tools indicate is a self-hosted wallet the customer controls. What is the correct operational handling?

options.text: Collect and, on a risk basis, verify the required beneficiary information for the self-hosted wallet, since there is no counterparty VASP to exchange data with.
options.why: Self-hosted destinations shift the obligation to gathering customer-side beneficiary information.

options.text: Refuse the withdrawal outright, because Travel Rule transfers can only ever be sent to another registered VASP and never to self-hosted wallets.
options.why: The Travel Rule does not ban self-hosted-wallet withdrawals; it requires appropriate handling.

options.text: Send a full IVMS 101 message to the destination address itself and wait for the wallet to acknowledge receipt of the originator and beneficiary data before releasing any funds.
options.why: A self-hosted wallet is not a VASP and cannot receive or acknowledge IVMS 101 messages.

options.text: Treat it as a standard VASP-to-VASP transfer and query counterparty directories for the institution that operates the address.
options.why: No operating institution controls a self-hosted wallet, so counterparty lookup does not apply.

spec.prompt: Sort each piece of investigation evidence into on-chain or off-chain.

items.label: Blockchain-analytics tracing showing the deposit came within two hops of a sanctioned cluster.
items.why: Ledger tracing of hops between addresses is on-chain evidence.

items.label: The customer's KYC file and stated source of funds recorded at onboarding.
items.why: Identity and onboarding records live off the ledger in firm systems.

items.label: A cluster attribution linking the destination address to a known illicit-services operator.
items.why: Address attribution is derived from on-chain data and analytics.

items.label: Support-ticket messages where the customer explained the purpose of a transfer.
items.why: Customer communications are off-chain, firm-held records.

items.label: The transaction hash, timestamps, and amounts recorded on the ledger.
items.why: Transaction details written to the ledger are on-chain evidence.

spec.prompt: Put the steps from monitoring alert to regulatory filing in the correct order.

steps.label: Triage the alert to confirm it reflects genuinely unusual activity rather than a false positive.
steps.why: Triage separates noise from cases worth a full investigation.

steps.label: Investigate by gathering on-chain tracing and off-chain customer evidence.
steps.why: Evidence-building establishes what actually happened before any conclusion.

steps.label: Reach and document a reasoned suspicion determination.
steps.why: A documented determination is the basis for deciding whether to report.

steps.label: File a suspicious activity report with the relevant financial intelligence unit within the required timeframe.
steps.why: Timely filing to the FIU completes the reporting obligation.

title: The customer asks about a delay

spec.prompt: After a suspicious activity report is filed, the customer contacts support asking why a withdrawal is delayed. An analyst wants to keep the customer informed. What is the correct handling?

options.text: Do not disclose that a report has been filed or that an investigation is underway; respond only within approved, neutral service messaging.
options.why: Withholding the report and investigation avoids tipping off while maintaining lawful contact.

options.text: Explain that a suspicious activity report was filed so the customer can provide documents to clear the matter quickly.
options.why: Revealing that a report was filed is prohibited tipping off.

options.text: Tell the customer the account is under review by the financial intelligence unit and share the report reference number so they can follow up on the status themselves.
options.why: Disclosing the FIU review and a report reference is a clear tipping-off breach.

options.text: Close the account immediately without any communication, since any contact with the customer after filing is strictly prohibited.
options.why: Neutral service contact is allowed; abrupt closure can itself signal a filing.

spec.prompt: Sort each activity at a crypto exchange into the line of defence that owns it.

buckets.label: First line (owns and runs the control)

items.label: An onboarding analyst screens a new customer's deposit address and clears the account
items.why: Running a day-to-day control on live activity is a first-line task.

items.label: The compliance team writes the wallet-screening policy and reviews the desk's exception reports
items.why: Setting policy and providing oversight and challenge is second-line work.

items.label: Internal audit tests whether the desk actually followed the wallet-screening policy over the past year
items.why: Independent testing of control effectiveness for the board is third-line assurance.

items.label: The MLRO decides whether to file a suspicious activity report after an escalation
items.why: The MLRO sits in the second line and owns the reporting decision.

items.label: The withdrawals operations team applies the daily transaction limits set out in policy
items.why: Applying controls in operations is first-line ownership of the risk.

spec.prompt: A business team wants to launch a new privacy-focused token pairing next week to beat competitors. The board's approved risk appetite statement says the firm will not onboard products whose AML controls have not been independently assessed. What should the board do?

options.text: Hold the launch until the second line assesses the AML control design and independent testing confirms the controls work, in line with the approved risk appetite.
options.why: Board oversight means enforcing the risk appetite even under commercial pressure.

options.text: Approve the launch immediately because speed to market is a commercial priority, and instruct compliance to retrofit monitoring rules and complete the control assessment at some point after customers have already begun trading the new pairing.
options.why: Launching before assessment breaches the stated risk appetite and inverts control.

options.text: Delegate the whole go or no-go decision to the first-line business team, since they best understand the product.
options.why: The board cannot delegate away its accountability for AML risk.

options.text: Approve the launch but switch off transaction monitoring on the new pairing to keep false positives low.
options.why: Disabling monitoring removes the very control the appetite requires.

spec.prompt: Match each part of the governance structure to the responsibility it holds.

pairs.right: Owns and manages the risk in day-to-day operations
pairs.why: The business units run and own the controls.

pairs.right: Sets AML policy, provides challenge, and owns the reporting decision
pairs.why: Compliance oversees the first line and the MLRO decides on reports.

pairs.right: Provides independent assurance to the board on control effectiveness
pairs.why: Audit is independent of the functions it reviews.

pairs.right: Sets the risk appetite and holds ultimate accountability for the program
pairs.why: Ultimate governance accountability rests with the board.

spec.scenario: An exchange outsources most transaction screening to a single blockchain-analytics vendor and relies on the vendor's output without much internal checking.
spec.prompt: Which of the following are genuine red flags a reviewer should raise?

items.label: A privileged compliance-operations employee repeatedly reopens and closes alerts for one cluster of accounts outside normal hours
items.why: Unusual privileged access to case data suggests an insider threat.

items.label: The firm has never tested the vendor's screening output against a known set of sanctioned addresses and accepts results without validation
items.why: Accepting vendor output unchecked is over-reliance and an assurance failure.

items.label: The firm rotates its independent testing to a different qualified team each cycle and documents the scope
items.why: Documented, independent, rotating testing is sound practice.

items.label: The vendor's attribution data has known coverage gaps on newer chains, and there is no compensating manual review
items.why: Uncovered analytics limits with no compensating control leave blind spots.

items.label: New monitoring hires pass a role-specific competency check before handling live alerts
items.why: Verified training effectiveness is a control strength, not a flaw.

spec.prompt: The board wants assurance over the AML program. The head of AML compliance offers to run the review herself, since she knows the controls best. What is the correct approach?

options.text: Have the review performed by a party independent of the AML function, such as internal audit or a qualified external firm, so the assurance is objective.
options.why: Independent testing requires a reviewer separate from the function assessed.

options.text: Let the head of AML compliance run the review because she has the deepest knowledge of the controls, and simply ask her to note in the report that she designed and operated many of the controls she is now assessing so readers understand the context.
options.why: Self-review is not independent, and a disclosure note does not cure it.

options.text: Skip the independent test this cycle because the controls were tested when first built.
options.why: Assurance must be periodic, not a one-time event.

options.text: Ask the first-line onboarding team to review the AML controls instead.
options.why: The first line owns the controls and cannot independently assure them.

spec.prompt: Put the steps for onboarding and governing a new blockchain-analytics vendor in the correct order.

steps.label: Perform risk-based due diligence on the vendor, covering data coverage, security, and ownership
steps.why: Due diligence establishes whether the vendor is fit before any commitment.

steps.label: Agree contractual service levels, data-quality expectations, and audit rights
steps.why: Contract terms lock in accountability before go-live.

steps.label: Independently validate the vendor's output against known test cases before going live
steps.why: Validation confirms the tool works before reliance begins.

steps.label: Schedule ongoing performance monitoring and periodic re-validation of results
steps.why: Vendor risk is continuous and must be reviewed over time.

spec.prompt: Match each emerging concept to the description that best fits it.

pairs.right: Moves value between blockchains, breaking a single-chain trace and forcing analysts to follow value across ledgers
pairs.why: Bridges fragment the audit trail across networks.

pairs.right: A central-bank-issued digital form of sovereign currency, distinct from a decentralized cryptoasset
pairs.why: A CBDC is state-issued, not a permissionless token.

pairs.right: Represents ownership of an off-chain asset as an on-chain token, linking real-world value to a ledger
pairs.why: Tokenisation ties a physical or financial asset to a chain.

pairs.right: A member-governed on-chain organization where control may run through governance tokens rather than a company
pairs.why: A DAO distributes control across token holders.

spec.prompt: A lending protocol is marketed as fully autonomous, but a small founding team holds admin keys and can change fees, pause the protocol, and control the treasury. A regulator is assessing whether AML obligations apply. What is the most defensible position?

options.text: Where identifiable persons keep control or sufficient influence over the service, they can fall within scope as an obliged entity despite the decentralized label.
options.why: Substance over label: retained control can create obligations.

options.text: Because the protocol describes itself as decentralized and runs on smart contracts, no natural or legal person can be treated as an obliged entity, so AML obligations cannot attach to anyone involved in launching, maintaining, or profiting from the service.
options.why: A label does not remove obligations where real control persists.

options.text: The protocol is automatically exempt because software cannot be a regulated person.
options.why: Obligations attach to the people behind the software, not the code.

options.text: AML obligations apply only to the users who borrow and lend, never to the operators.
options.why: Operators with control can themselves be in scope.

spec.scenario: A firm is horizon-scanning risks across a new DeFi and cross-chain product line.
spec.prompt: Which observations are genuine emerging AFC risk indicators to escalate?

items.label: Customer inflows are routinely routed through a cross-chain bridge and a mixing service immediately before reaching the firm
items.why: Layered bridge-and-mixer routing obscures fund origin.

items.label: A counterparty protocol claims decentralization but one team controls upgrades and can redirect user funds
items.why: Concentrated hidden control is an emerging governance risk.

items.label: The firm publishes a periodic horizon-scanning report reviewed by the second line
items.why: Structured horizon scanning is good governance, not a risk.

items.label: Tokenised real-world assets are onboarded with no check on the off-chain asset's provenance or issuer
items.why: Unverified RWA provenance can mask illicit value on-chain.

items.label: A customer uses a mainstream, well-attributed exchange with full KYC for fiat off-ramps
items.why: A transparent, KYC'd off-ramp is not itself a red flag.

spec.prompt: Attackers have drained a hot wallet and are moving stolen funds on-chain. Put the response steps in the correct order.

steps.label: Contain the incident: isolate affected systems and halt further outflows
steps.why: Stopping ongoing loss comes first.

steps.label: Trace the stolen funds on-chain and identify off-ramp chokepoints
steps.why: Tracing locates where value can still be intercepted.

steps.label: Notify authorities, file required reports, and request freezing at cooperating exchanges
steps.why: Reporting and freezing requests enable recovery and compliance.

steps.label: Run a post-incident review and feed lessons learned back into controls
steps.why: Learning from the event builds resilience.

spec.scenario: A monitoring team reviews signals during a suspected security incident at a custodial wallet service.
spec.prompt: Which observations warrant treating this as an active incident and escalating?

items.label: A large batch of withdrawals to newly created external addresses executes far outside normal patterns and volumes
items.why: Abnormal mass outflows signal a possible drain.

items.label: The outgoing funds are being split and routed through a mixer and a cross-chain bridge
items.why: Rapid layering of outflows indicates laundering of stolen funds.

items.label: Scheduled maintenance produced a brief, expected dip in successful logins overnight
items.why: A planned, explained dip is benign.

items.label: An internal admin account approves withdrawals from an unusual location at an unusual hour
items.why: Anomalous admin behavior suggests compromise or insider abuse.

items.label: The daily reconciliation of hot-wallet balances matches the ledger exactly
items.why: A clean reconciliation is reassuring, not a warning sign.

spec.prompt: Sort each action into whether it belongs to the active response or to post-incident resilience work.

items.label: Freeze internal withdrawal processing to stop further loss
items.why: Containment is an immediate response action.

items.label: File the required regulatory report and notify law enforcement
items.why: Prompt reporting is part of managing a live incident.

items.label: Update the incident-response playbook based on what went wrong
items.why: Revising the playbook is a lessons-learned activity.

items.label: Trace stolen funds to an off-ramp and ask the receiving exchange to freeze them
items.why: Tracing and freezing requests happen while funds are still recoverable.

items.label: Add new detection rules and strengthen key management uncovered by the review
items.why: Hardening controls afterward builds resilience.
